gaps: No seed-pod shot — the elongated capsules that split to self-sow are a notable feature called out in blurb/seasons/care; missing. Foliage-only detail is only incidentally visible alongside flowers. Habit shot shows a sparse young plant in dry scrub rather than a cheerful garden drift. Summer-only reel for a plant with a spring-early-fall season.

Missing a true whole-plant/habit shot showing the airy branching form and height (both shots are flowers only); the su-structure shot is a side-angled pair of reflexed flowers past peak, not a plant habit view; add a shot pulling back to show the ferny mound in bloom context.

Habit shot (su-habit) is a scrappy single plant by a wall — weak context; replace with a drift or fuller plant mid-bloom showing the airy mass. No dedicated foliage/ferny-leaf close-up (the foliage is a key ID feature). Seed-pod/needle-head shot useful but low priority (NA scored). Caption calls the flower 'pink ray' but both shots are magenta-purple — consider adding a white or bicolor form to represent the species range.

Shots are effectively inverted: su-close shows a full spike at medium distance (better as a habit/structure shot) while su-structure is an extreme macro of 2–3 hand-held flowers — unflattering and lacking context. Neither shot is a clean, front-facing close-up showing the characteristic spur detail and multiple florets in focus on a spike. Foliage (the distinctive ferny texture) is barely visible in either image. No whole-plant portrait showing the tall (3–4 ft) upright habit with multiple spikes. REPLACE both: need (1) a clean front-facing spike close-up showing several spurred florets in sharp focus, flowers not hand-held, and (2) a true habit shot of one or more full-height plants showing the ferny foliage and airy spike structure. A fall/self-sown seedling shot would be a bonus. The caption for su-close says 'blue-purple' but the image shows pale pink/lavender — caption color mismatch.

foliage: palmate compound leaves visible as background detail in all shots but never the primary subject; a dedicated foliage close-up showing the distinctive leaf shape would aid non-flowering identification

seed_pods: fa-seedpods shows an early/transitional stage — mostly flower buds with nascent pods just forming, not the 'slender dangling' mature pods described in the blurb and seasons field; a shot of ripe tan pods on the stem would complete the fall story

whole_plant: su-habit shows a good prairie stand in bloom but cuts off the tops of plants; a frame showing a full-height individual plant from base to tip against an open sky/prairie background would solidify the 2-4 ft height impression

REPLACE su-flower: the flower close-up is shot at a low angle in overcast light with a busy willow-foliage background that makes identification harder; seek a front-facing, well-lit spike isolated against a cleaner background.

REPLACE su-habit: the habit shot shows pale yellow/white cultivars with many spent (brown) pods visible — not visually appealing and gives a poor impression of the plant's colour range; replace with a vibrant multi-color clump in active bloom.
ADD: a front-facing close-up of a single fully open flower spike showing the characteristic 'snap' pouch shape clearly.
ADD: a spring or fall shot showing the cool-season rebloom, since the blurb emphasizes the two-flush bloom cycle.
ADD: a true front-facing eye-level shot of a single upright stem showing the full white-margined leaf pattern from mid-plant to top — current shots are either very macro or slightly top-down

REPLACE su-colony: soft focus on foreground plants and side-angle composition makes this the weakest of the three; replace with a sharper colony/naturalized-stand shot at eye level showing multiple upright stems
ADD: late-season shot when the whole plant is maximally white-frosted to illustrate the seasonal progression described in the blurb

No dedicated seed-spray shot: the oat-like seed heads visible as small wisps in su-close are never the subject; ADD a summer shot of the tall airy seed sprays rising above the clump — the single most distinctive ornamental feature per the blurb and care notes
No spring or winter shot: the blurb emphasizes semi-evergreen character year-round; a winter shot showing the bleached-but-still-architectural clump would complete the story
su-structure thumb shows a young, sparse, weedy-looking wild plant in rocky scree — adequate for habit but not flattering; consider REPLACING with a garden-grown clump showing the full fountain form

Summer clump thumbnail (su-clump-t.jpg) is weak: the plant is small, distant, and lower-right in a wide rocky scene — the grass reads as a minor detail, not a garden specimen; replace with a closer shot of a mature blue-green clump. No dedicated close-up of the characteristic fluffy white seedheads described as peak ornamental interest (the wi-seedhead shot shows the whole clump but not the seedhead detail). Fall clump reads as a dense mass, not a single identifiable plant.

The structure shot is a low-resolution phone photo with cluttered lichen/debris background; replace with a cleaner mat-carpet shot showing the groundcover effect (e.g. dense blue mat between pavers or on a slope); no shot of the dark-green evergreen foliage alone without flowers (winter-interest mat).

No dedicated succulent-foliage close-up showing the distinctive glaucous finger-like leaves without flowers (the 'evergreen mat in winter' claim has no image support). A winter or late-fall shot showing the mat without bloom would be valuable given the plant's year-round evergreen habit. Otherwise the two summer shots (macro flower + carpet habit) are strong.

Close-up (sp-close) is excellent — sharp, front-facing, shows the nodding pink-tipped urn flowers clearly against glossy leaves. Structure shot (su-structure) shows foliage and trailing habit at an intermediate scale which reads more like a foliage-only shot than a whole-plant portrait showing the full spreading mat; no sense of scale or mat extent. Missing: (1) a fall/winter shot showing the signature bright-red berries — this is a major selling point mentioned prominently in the blurb and seasons, and winter interest is flagged true; a berry shot is nearly mandatory. (2) A wider mat shot or winter/fall seasonal shot with bronze-purple leaf color. seed_pods scored 1 because berries exist and are notable but no shot covers them.

foliage (PRIORITY): the plant's defining character is its silver-gray WOOLLY mat — neither shot shows the silvery/woolly leaf texture; sp-close shows the flower face-on with blurry green foliage behind; sp-structure foliage appears green and somewhat pinnate (raising a possible wrong-ID concern for that shot — does not look like typical Cerastium tomentosum woolly mat); a dedicated shot of the silver mat WITHOUT flowers is needed

bloom_in_context: sp-structure shows only a small section of mat with 2–3 scattered flowers; the 'smothered in white star flowers' blurb effect is not conveyed; a wider shot of a large drift in peak bloom (the 'snow' carpet effect) is the primary missing shot type

thumb_framing: sp-structure-t crops tightly to the edge of the mat with only one centered flower and soil background; does not read as 'groundcover' at card scale; a wider or better-composed replacement would serve better
NICE-TO-HAVE: a late-season (fall) shot showing the fluffy seed heads, which the record notes are wind-dispersed and contribute to colony spread.
REPLACE su-close: overhead straight-down perspective gives no sense of flower form or face — replace with a front-facing, eye-level shot of one or two fully open red-and-gold flowers

REPLACE su-structure: shows two flower heads mostly past peak bloom (cone-shaped seed heads, few remaining petals) surrounded by tall grass — neither a useful habit/whole-plant shot nor a good bloom shot; replace with a clear image of a blooming plant in garden or meadow context showing the mounding form with multiple open flowers
ADD: a true whole-plant habit shot showing the clumping form in full summer bloom with foliage clearly visible
ADD: a dedicated foliage close-up showing the hairy, lance-shaped leaves (useful for identification)
ADD: winter seedhead shot — globose fluffy seedheads are noted as persisting into winter and feeding goldfinches
No fall foliage shot: the blurb and seasons field explicitly call out the crimson-red fall foliage as a key season-of-interest; ADD a fall shot showing the leaf color
Foliage detail is incidental (leaves visible but never the subject) — a dedicated foliage close-up showing the deeply cut, star-shaped leaves would strengthen identification

No whole-plant or habit shot at all — neither image shows the airy upright wand form that defines this plant; the 'structure' shot is actually a single-flower macro. The close-up flower color renders as deep violet-purple rather than the sky-blue described in the blurb — possible color accuracy issue worth verifying (Linum lewisii is sky blue, not deep indigo). The close-up thumbnail crops tightly on two blooms against blurry dry-stem background. Needs: a true whole-plant habit shot showing branching stems with multiple blooms; a foliage shot of the fine needle-like leaves.

The structure shot (sp-structure) is a weak phone snapshot from overhead showing only a small flower cluster among dead sticks in an unkempt setting — no plant habit, no leaf shape visible; the signature large heart-shaped (often silver-splashed) leaves are completely absent from both shots; need a foliage shot showing the ornamental leaf, and a whole-plant habit shot; replace sp-structure entirely.

Both shots are taken in the wild (leaf-litter/twig background, sparse plant), not a cultivated garden setting — the plant looks scrubby and doesn't convey the dense 'carpet smothered in white' described in the blurb. Need a shot of a full, well-established mat in full-bloom garden context. No foliage/winter shot showing the 'glossy dark evergreen foliage year-round' that is called out as a key trait. The sp-structure thumb framing shows decent habit but the plant is visually sparse compared to a cultivated specimen.

Flower close-up (su-flower) is outstanding — perfectly front-facing, one bloom centered, blue-and-white coloration crisp, stamens detailed. Habit shot (su-habit) is good: multiple blooms, buds, and ferny foliage visible, realistic wildflower-meadow context. Gaps: neither shot shows the full basal clump of ferny leaves (foliage scored 1 — partially visible but not the focus); no shot gives a sense of the whole plant from base to tip. No fall or winter shot (plant goes dormant — not essential but optional). The habit shot is slightly busy/cluttered with grasses and other wildflowers which make it harder to read as 'one plant.' A clean whole-plant portrait in garden context would round out the reel.
flowers: su-close is the only flower close-up and the specimen has visibly blemished/brown-spotted petals; replace with a fresh, undamaged single flower in crisp focus

foliage: the narrow lance-shaped to pinnate leaves (a key identification feature distinguishing Coreopsis from other yellow daisies) are completely absent from both shots — su-close is a petal-filling macro with no foliage; su-structure shows stems and flowers but leaves are background blur only; add a shot showing clear leaf form
whole_plant: su-structure is a mid-crop of many stems and flowers in a drift — good for bloom context but does not isolate a single clump to show the 18–24 in mounding growth habit
thumb_framing: su-close-t is cropped so tightly that petals are clipped on multiple sides of the frame; even at 720×480 the single flower is not fully contained

CRITICAL: both shots are of a single young stem/rosette at ground level in a moist woodland setting — this contradicts the plant's identity as a 'tidy chartreuse dome' in full sun on lean, dry soil. Neither shot shows the characteristic cushion/mound habit.

REPLACE sp-flowers: the flowers close-up (top-down of one stem) does show the chartreuse bracts but from an unconventional overhead angle and does not read as a garden ornamental; seek a side-angle shot of the full dome in bloom.
REPLACE sp-habit: the 'habit' shot shows a single small plant growing in dense wet shade next to ferns — wrong habitat, wrong scale, wrong context for a full-sun xeric subshrub.
ADD: a shot of the full mounded plant showing the characteristic cushion silhouette at peak spring bloom — this is the plant's defining visual feature.
ADD: a fall shot showing the red-orange-purple autumn foliage colour, which the blurb calls out as the plant's 'second season of interest'.

ADD: a clear whole-plant/habit shot showing a single dahlia plant's bushy form, height, and staking — the fa-habit shot shows multiple plants in a mixed border but the species' own form is not the focus
ADD: a dedicated foliage close-up showing the distinctive compound/deeply lobed dark-green leaves — currently only glimpsed behind the flower in su-flower
ADD: a shot showing flower diversity (ideally a single vs. semi-double or dinnerplate type) to reflect the cultivar range mentioned in the blurb and care notes
ADD: a summer context shot showing dahlias in a garden border at their peak in July–August bloom season

su-close (labeled 'close') shows a sparse wild plant photographed from above at an angle with only 1–2 flowers visible — NOT a proper close-up of the fringed flower; foliage shown is a basal rosette with broad strap leaves, not the narrow blue-gray grassy mat described in the blurb — this may be a misidentified specimen or a juvenile; REPLACE with a proper front-facing flower close-up showing fringed pink petals

su-structure (labeled 'structure') shows the same plant wedged in rock crevice at an oblique angle — provides no sense of the mounded blue-gray mat habit; REPLACE with a shot of an established mat smothered in bloom
Both shots show a wild cliff-dwelling plant, not the garden groundcover form the blurb describes — the silver-blue mat and clove-scented density are completely absent; both shots need replacing
No shot shows the characteristic silver-blue evergreen mat foliage; ADD a winter or off-season foliage shot to demonstrate the year-round evergreen interest called out in seasons and winter=true
Thumb framing on su-close-t: the diagonal composition centers on rock with the plant at the lower edge — poor use of the card crop

The close-up (su-close) is heavily backlit and oblique — petals are blown-out and clipped at top, no front-facing view of the full cup; worst thumbnail in the batch. Structure shot shows a single plant at a pavement edge among dead vegetation — not a garden plant. The four-winged reddish seedpods are explicitly called out in the blurb as a KEY feature and are completely absent from the reel. Neither shot conveys the sprawling groundcover character. Needs: a clean front-facing flower shot; a pod shot; a wider habit shot showing the low-spreading mound.

Both shots focus on flower spikes; no dedicated foliage shot showing the distinctive bold palmate leaves at ground level; no clearly front-facing single-spike close-up (su-close shows a spike in a meadow from a slight angle, partially obscured by adjacent stems); a close frontal shot of open florets would sharpen ID value.

Major gaps across all axes. Flower shot (sp-flowers): only single/semi-double white blooms — peonies are famous for double, bomb, or anemone-form blowsy blooms; the shot under-sells the plant. No front-facing clean flower portrait. The summer foliage shot (su-foliage) shows a small, unimpressive seedling-scale plant lying low in leaf litter with weedy surroundings — this does not represent a mature peony clump and should be replaced. No whole-plant shot showing a mature clump at bloom peak. No fall bronzing shot. Thumb framing on su-foliage-t is especially weak (tiny plant lost in dead leaves). This is one of the weakest entries in the batch.

Close-up panicle (su-close) is excellent — large, dense domed head in deep crimson-pink fills the frame, well lit. Structure shot (su-structure) shows a full flowering stem in lavender-purple with leaves visible down the stem — good habit framing. However: (1) the two shots show very different cultivar colors (deep crimson vs. lavender-purple) with no indication they are intentionally showing color diversity — could confuse users about what 'garden phlox' looks like; ideally both show the same or a clearly labeled mix. (2) No whole-plant shot showing the multi-stemmed 3-ft clump. (3) Foliage (the lance-shaped leaves which are important for powdery mildew ID) is only incidentally visible. (4) No fall or post-bloom shot. Adding a labeled multi-color drift or a single-cultivar set would strengthen species identity.

bloom_in_context: sp-structure shows 3–4 scattered flowers in autumn leaf-litter with a stick across the frame; does not convey the 'carpets the ground' colony effect the plant is prized for; replace with a shot of an established spring drift (10+ flowers naturalized across a woodland floor or under a shrub) to match the blurb
whole_plant: both shots are ground-level close views; no shot shows the broader naturalized colony habit from a pulled-back perspective
foliage: the deeply divided ferny leaves are partially visible in sp-close but are not the subject; a clearer shot where the leaves are identifiable alongside a bloom would help field ID
note: both shots are from the same photographer (Kostas Zontanos) and appear to be from the same observation session; broadening to a second source would reduce single-observer dependency

su-close (the first/primary shot) shows the flower at an angle with partial petals hidden; the flower appears washed-out pale lavender rather than the violet-blue with white eye described in the blurb. The card thumbnail crops to just petals and stamens at an angle. Ideally replace with a fully front-facing flower clearly showing the white eye and violet-blue colouration.

REPLACE or ADD whole-plant/habit: neither shot shows the 'mounding and sprawling groundcover' habit. A shot of the full plant mound covered in flowers — its defining garden asset — is entirely absent.
ADD: a foliage shot showing the distinctive deeply lobed cranesbill leaves, which also show attractive fall red-orange colour per the record.
ADD: a late-summer or fall shot showing fall colour or the sprawling habit post-shear and rebloom.

ADD (critical): a whole-plant / mat-forming habit shot showing the spreading rhizomatous colony from a pulled-back perspective — the defining character of this plant (low spreading mat on rocky or dry soil) is completely absent; both current shots are stem-level close-ups of essentially the same thing
ADD: a wider context shot showing the plant in its native rocky hillside or dry-slope habitat to communicate its xeric, groundcover use case

fa-close shows the white flower held in a human hand — the flower itself is excellent and front-facing, but the hand dominates the frame and obscures the plant context; REPLACE with an unhandled front-facing flower shot

No whole-plant / habit shot: fa-structure shows a flower stem cluster (essentially a second flower close-up, different cultivar/color) — there is no shot showing the tall, wiry-stemmed 2.5–4 ft colony habit the blurb describes; ADD a wide habit shot
No foliage detail: the bold, dark-green palmate leaves (a key identification feature) are only background blur; ADD a dedicated leaf shot
Two shots both show only flowers (no stems-in-context, no habit) — only one season represented

No whole-plant or habit shot — the wiry branching stems weaving through a border (the plant's defining garden character per the blurb) are not shown; the 'structure' shot shows one flower head in a mixed border, not the plant itself. No foliage shot. Needs a mid-distance shot showing the airy, wiry-stemmed plant form. Color rendering in close-up reads magenta-pink rather than wine-red/burgundy.

Both shots are from the same plant against the same rock backdrop and show only 1-2 flowers; no wide habit shot showing the spreading clumping form with multiple stems and flowers (12-24 in mound); the silver-woolly stems and foliage appear in the close-up but a dedicated wider view of the plant in a garden setting is missing.

No foliage shot at all — the distinctive hairy, pinnately cut basal leaves and the fall re-emergence rosette are both unrepresented, and summer dormancy (the gap left in the border) is a noted ID/care feature. The habit shot shows blooms + nodding fuzzy buds + grass but the plant is cropped off at the top (camera shoots from below, flowers partially cut). Seed capsule is incidentally visible in the habit shot (counts as 1 rather than 2 — it's not the focus). A dedicated shot of the ornamental seed pods would be highly valuable. Consider a fall rosette shot to illustrate the plant's re-emergence.

Both shots show the plant from above/oblique at medium-far distance — neither provides a close-up of individual flowers (the small white-to-pale-lilac daisy with yellow center is barely legible). fa-close is an overhead shot of a tangled colony — not a true close-up at all; it functions more as a colony/mass habit shot but the framing is poor (overhead, no clear subject focus). fa-structure is slightly better but shot from above at an angle into low, weedy vegetation; the plant stems and flowers are mixed with surrounding grasses and other species. Thumb framing for fa-close-t crops into the overhead colony shot with no clear focal point. REPLACE fa-close with a genuine close-up: one to three daisy heads, front-facing, showing the ray-floret-plus-yellow-disk detail. The structure shot could be improved with a clear upright portrait of a single tall stem or small colony from a lower angle. Seed pods/seedheads are mentioned in seasons ('feathery seedheads persist') — a shot would be useful but not critical. The willow-like lance leaves are the key ID feature for summer foliage but are barely visible in either shot.

front_facing / open flower (PRIORITY): sp-close shows the flower from the side/back as a nearly closed bud — the iconic silky lavender cup open wide with golden stamens fully visible is completely absent; this is the plant's primary identity feature and the card's first-slot thumbnail is a bud-from-below view that conveys almost nothing about the open flower

whole_plant-in-bloom (PRIORITY): there is no shot of the plant at flowering time at all; sp-structure contains only seedheads with blurry foliage behind — no shot shows the fuzzy stems pushing up through prairie grass with open cups as described in the blurb
foliage: the distinctive silky-hairy ferny basal leaves are entirely absent from both shots; they appear only as background blur in sp-structure
whole_plant: score 0 — no shot shows the whole plant form (compact clump with leaves + flowers or plumes together at once); sp-close is a single stem/bud and sp-structure is a seedhead macro

consider: the seedhead shot (sp-structure) is excellent and should be kept; relabeling it 'sp-seedheads' and adding an open-flower close-up plus a whole-plant habit shot would bring this reel from poor to good

su-close thumbnail is misleadingly labelled — it is actually a wide overhead shot of a low-mounding plant covered in pink clusters (good 'whole plant' angle but not a close-up); the 'structure' shot is actually a tight close-up of a flower cluster showing the individual florets. The shots are swapped in their roles.

The plant is described as having 'dense rose-red' clusters and 'glaucous blue-gray foliage' — both shots show pink (not red) flowers, and the signature blue-gray foliage is not showcased. A shot clearly showing the glaucous blue-gray stem and leaf colour alongside the flowers would strengthen identification.
ADD: a true whole-plant habit shot showing the full upright clump (18–36 in tall) in a rock wall or lean-soil context, which is the plant's signature garden habitat.
ADD: a close-up of a single individual flower cluster (head-on, showing the tubular individual flowers) to serve as a proper identification close-up.

REPLACE sp-habit: shows a single small plant squeezed against a concrete curb edge with dead mulch — conveys an unpleasant setting rather than the plant's typical garden use; replace with a clean whole-clump shot in a border or open garden setting showing the mounded form with multiple upright violet spikes
ADD: a foliage close-up showing the gray-green, wrinkled, aromatic leaves — the sp-habit shot gives only a very small glimpse and su-flower clips foliage almost entirely
ADD: a bloom-in-context shot showing a drift of multiple plants together (the plant is best used in groups of 3+) to show its full garden impact

su-close is shot from the BACK of the flowerhead showing the calyx and bracts — interesting botanically but not front-facing; shows the underside rather than the lavender-blue face and protruding stamens; REPLACE with a front-facing flower close-up

su-structure shows a single flower stem rising from a meadow grass sward — the plant itself (the compact ferny-foliage mound) is completely absent from frame; the basal rosette/mound habit and ferny gray-green foliage are the identifying features of the cultivated garden plant; REPLACE with a shot showing the garden plant's mounding foliage habit
No foliage shot: the gray-green ferny leaves are never shown; ADD a foliage detail
su-structure-t thumb is cropped so the flower head is at the very top edge of frame, mostly showing the wiry stem and background meadow — poor framing for card use

The flower close-up (su-flower) is a side/profile shot of a partially-spent bloom against a grey background — the flower is past peak with dark browning bracts, and the 'scarlet' color reads as hot pink/magenta not true red; not front-facing. The habit shot thumbnail reads as a meadow scene at distance with no clearly identifiable individual plant. No foliage shot. No seedhead shot (blurb notes 'seed heads & stems hold into winter'). Top priority: a front-facing shot of a fresh scarlet mop-head on a healthy plant.

Minor: no dedicated foliage/basal rosette shot (low priority since the plant is known for its flowers); could benefit from a single stem close-up showing the dark foliage against white petals, but the current two shots cover the rubric well.

All three shots are technically acceptable but none are excellent. Spring foliage shot: extreme overhead look-down at tiny seedlings in rocky/leaf-litter soil — doesn't convey the silvery palmate leaf well (poor light, small scale, no silver sheen visible). Flower close-up: shot from below/side in strong backlighting, partially out of focus top of spike — needs a front-facing spike portrait showing the full violet-blue raceme cleanly. Habit shot: pale/washed-out colors, muddy background, plants look weedy. No shot specifically isolates the 'silvery' leaf texture that gives the plant its name and is its most distinctive feature. Seed pods explicitly mentioned as toxic and notable — no pod shot. Thumb framing on sp-foliage-t and su-habit-t are both weak (look-down overhead vs. washed-out low-contrast).

Critical concern: su-close (labeled 'White flower spikes — Icicle type') appears to show elongated tubular 4-petaled flowers on a sparse spike more consistent with a different genus (possibly Veronicastrum or a lookalike) — the flowers look much larger and more tubular than the tiny starry Veronica spicata florets expected. Needs species ID verification. su-structure (the 'clump of white spikes') is dominated by a large bumblebee in close-up, which nearly entirely obscures the spikes — the plant is barely visible. No whole-plant shot showing the basal rosette and vertical spikes together. No front-facing true close-up of the characteristic dense, starry spike. Foliage (dark green serrate leaves) is incidentally visible in su-close but not featured. Both images are from Wikimedia Commons (David J. Stang), which is fine for licensing, but the plant ID on su-close should be re-verified. REPLACE su-structure with a clear plant portrait — remove the bee-dominated close-up as the only 'structure' shot. ADD a whole-plant shot showing the clumping habit with upright spikes.

caption/label mismatch (ACTION REQUIRED): su-close.jpg (caption: 'Dense rose-purple flower cluster') actually shows the whole trailing mat from above; su-structure.jpg (caption: 'Trailing mat') actually shows a single flower cluster close-up — the two images are swapped relative to their captions/slugnames

flowers: su-structure (the actual flower close-up) shows a cluster that is strongly backlit and slightly out-of-focus; individual florets are small; a well-lit face-on shot of the flat-topped 2–3 in flower cluster showing individual 5-petaled florets clearly would be a significant upgrade
foliage: the coarsely toothed, hairy leaves of Glandularia are visible in su-structure but as minor background; a dedicated foliage shot would help ID

whole_plant: the mat view (su-close, labeled as close) is actually an excellent overhead habitat shot showing the spreading purple bloom coverage — this functions well as bloom_in_context and whole_plant both; the habitat viewpoint is straight down rather than at an angle, which somewhat flattens the trailing/spreading impression

sp-close: the close-up shows orange flowers but the shot is taken at a downward angle showing a partly open/drooping cluster — not a clean front-facing head-on identification image. The blurb says 'flat-topped clusters of fragrant orange-to-yellow flowers'; a clean top-down or front-facing shot of a full flattened corymb would be ideal.

sp-structure: shows yellow-flowered plants (different colour from the sp-close which is orange), which highlights the natural colour variation but may confuse users. The structure shot is very small-scale (many small plants in grass) and gives no sense of individual plant architecture.

The plant's seasons entry notes 'narrow silique seed pods elongate and dry through summer' — seed pods are NOTABLE for a mustard-family plant that self-sows freely. ADD a summer shot showing elongated silique pods.
ADD: a shot of the basal rosette (the overwintering form), which is the plant's fall/winter presentation and an important identification feature for a biennial.

ADD (critical): a whole-plant habit shot showing the mounding prairie plant smothered in the described 'blizzard of tiny white daisies' — neither current shot shows the full plant form or the density-of-bloom effect that defines this species
ADD: winter seedhead shot — the plant has 'persistent feathery seedheads well into winter' and is flagged winter=true; this feature is entirely unrepresented
ADD: foliage close-up showing the small, linear heath-like leaves (ericoides = heath-like) that distinguish this from other asters

fa-close: flowers are slightly past peak — several ray florets are reflexed/drooping and disk centers show rust/pink (post-pollination aging); a fresher open-flower close-up would be stronger, though acceptable
No dedicated foliage shot: fine-textured grayish-green hairy leaves (a key ID feature distinguishing from other asters) are only visible in passing; a foliage close-up would help identification
No summer shot showing foliage before bloom — the plant is present in the garden all season; a pre-bloom foliage shot would complete the seasonal story

No dedicated foliage/stem detail shot showing the mint-family opposite leaves and square stems (an ID feature); the winter seedhead thumbnail is a single isolated dried head — evocative but a shot showing multiple seedheads standing together would be stronger. Overall the reel is the best in this batch.

Minor: neither shot is a straight front-facing macro of a single flat corymb head (both are top-down or angled); shot file names are effectively swapped (the 'close' file is the wide plant view and the 'structure' file is the close-up), which is cosmetically confusing but does not affect the reel.

Three strong shots covering habit-in-bloom, panicle close-up, and summer foliage with seed capsules. Main weakness: the summer foliage shot (su-foliage) shows a tropical-looking shrub with large oblong leaves and numerous yellow-green elongated capsules — this may NOT be Syringa vulgaris; the leaves are wrong (too large, ovate-lanceolate vs. the heart-shaped leaf expected), the capsules look like a different genus entirely. Needs verification that su-foliage.jpg is actually common lilac and not a misidentified observation. Thumb framing on sp-habit-t (the whole-shrub shot) is slightly soft/hazy due to overcast flat light, acceptable but not crisp.

Strong reel for two shots. sp-close shows yellow flower clusters with holly-like leaves clearly visible — flowers slightly bunched together and viewed from a slightly oblique angle rather than perfectly front-facing, but recognizable. sp-structure is an excellent full-mat shot showing the spreading low habit among rocks. Gaps: (1) No fall/winter shot showing the blue-purple berries (notable — plant has 'winter: true' and blurb prominently mentions dusty blue berries and winter bronze-purple leaf color — these are major selling points not shown). (2) Flowers in sp-close are buds/nearly-open rather than fully open — a shot with fully open yellow flowers would be preferable. seed_pods scored 1 because blue berries exist and are notable but unrepresented. Adding a fall/berry shot would elevate to 'good+' or 'excellent'.

whole_plant (PRIORITY): su-structure shows a portion of the shrub but the image is heavily overexposed/backlit — white flowers are washed out, shrub form is lost against a cluttered rocky/vegetated background; a properly exposed shot of the full 6–9 ft arching vase-shaped shrub in bloom from outside at distance is the most urgent replacement

bloom_in_context: the blurb describes 'billowy clusters of pure-white four-petaled flowers blanketing the arching branches' — no current shot shows the full shrub smothered in bloom; su-close is a branch fragment with a few clusters; su-structure is overexposed and confusing; want both shots to be strong
front_facing: su-close shows flowers at a slight oblique looking along a branch; a true face-on view of an open flower cluster (ideally multiple open flowers in one clean frame) would be stronger
foliage: the distinctive opposite ovate leaves with prominent veins are secondary background in both shots; a dedicated foliage shot would help identification outside of bloom
seasons: plant has winter stem structure ('arching vase-shaped stems provide winter structure' in seasons field) and fall color — both seasons are entirely absent from the 2-shot reel

wi-stems: the winter stem shot (the plant's signature feature) shows the red stems adequately but with a distracting building/structure background — a natural or garden background would be more appealing. The thumbnail framing is acceptable but leaves room for improvement.

fa-berries: this shot is actually showing late-season foliage in red-orange with a single flower cyme (not berries) — the label and cap say 'berries' but the shot does not clearly show white berries. The foliage colour is shown but the berries (a key wildlife feature) are barely present or absent from this shot.
ADD: a clean whole-plant shot showing the full shrub form, either in summer with foliage or in winter showing the full stem silhouette — neither exists currently.
ADD: a dedicated white-berry shot (the record calls out 'white berries' as a wildlife feature), distinct from the fall foliage shot.
NICE-TO-HAVE: a spring shot showing the flat white flower cymes more prominently against clean green foliage (the su-flowers shot is close but tightly cropped).

ADD (critical): a close-up of the fully developed feathery smoke plumes — the su-close shot shows pre-bloom flower buds and purple leaves only; the plant's entire identity is the fluffy inflorescence hairs and this defining feature is absent from the reel
ADD: fall color shot — smoke tree is described as having 'some of the most vivid orange-to-scarlet fall color of any Front Range shrub' and winter=true; no fall or winter shot exists

REPLACE su-close (or relabel): current su-close is a foliage + bud detail that works as a foliage shot but is misleadingly labeled as the 'close' feature shot for a plant whose main attraction is the smoke plumes; consider adding a true plume close-up and keeping the bud/leaf shot as a secondary foliage shot

NO FLOWER SHOT at all: the yellow tubular paired flowers are the primary bloom feature (May–Jul) and are completely absent from the reel; this is a critical gap for a plant named for its distinctive flower-and-bract pairing; ADD a summer flower shot showing the paired yellow tubular blooms in their leafy green bracts

su-close shows the twin berries/bracts held in a human hand — the berries+bracts detail is excellent and distinctive, but a hand-held shot is not ideal for the card; a shot on-plant showing the berries cradled in their red bracts on the branch would be stronger

su-structure shows a juvenile single-stemmed seedling-sized plant with berries at the base — NOT representative of the 4–10 ft × 4–8 ft mature shrub habit; REPLACE with a shot of an established multi-stem shrub
su-structure-t thumb frames mostly large leaves with the berries at bottom-center, very small — poor visual entry point for a card thumbnail
Missing spring shot: blurb leads with yellow flower racemes; that's what draws hummingbirds; ADD a spring/early-summer flower shot

No whole-shrub shot — the habit shot is a low ground-level close of a single flower among low shrubs; fails entirely to convey a 2-6 ft shrub that forms thickets. No shot shows multiple flowers on the shrub or a shrub in bloom. The winter hip shot (wi-hip-t.jpg) is a single shriveled hip at extreme close range in snow with a very busy background — reads poorly as a thumbnail and barely communicates what it is showing. Fall hip shot shows 1-2 hips, not a branch loaded with hips. Needs: a wide shot of the shrub in summer bloom; a winter shot of hips en masse on bare canes.

Neither shot shows the plant's defining silvery mound form: su-close is a single faded/partially-spent spike (flowers past peak, washed-out color); su-structure is a tight pre-bloom bud spike with no foliage or mound visible at all; need a whole-plant shot of the rounded silver-gray mound with purple spikes in full bloom, and the close-up should show a fully open spike at peak color; replace both shots.

Habit shot (su-habit) is good — shows the compact silvery mound with mauve bracts at a natural desert site. Flower close-up (su-flowers) shows the violet-and-mauve bract detail but flowers are largely spent/wilted (several flowers clearly past peak, drooping stamens) — the shot should show fresh, open violet blooms with healthy bracts, not an end-of-season state. No dedicated foliage shot isolating the thick silvery aromatic leaves that define this plant and give it its Latin name (pachyphylla = thick-leaved). No fall/winter shot showing the 'lasting mauve bracts' and 'winter structure' that are highlighted as key selling points.

su-close shows a hand holding/pulling a stem of yellow tubular flowers out from the plant — the hand is prominent and distracting, the flowers are at an awkward angle, and the shot conveys the flowers poorly (many are wilted or past peak). This is the worst close-up framing in the batch. su-structure is better — dense yellow flower mass on needle foliage, good color, shows the plant in bloom — but it's a top-down/oblique view into a massed planting, not a true portrait of a single specimen showing the subshrub habit. No clean front-facing close-up of a fresh tubular flower cluster. No shot showing the distinctive needle-like (pine-leaf) foliage detail that defines this species. No whole-plant portrait showing the compact mound form. REPLACE su-close with a genuine flower close-up (no hands), showing 3–5 tubular flowers from the front or slight angle with needle foliage visible. Improve or add a whole-plant portrait. The 'Mersea Yellow' cultivar's soft butter-yellow is actually well-captured colorwise in su-structure, so that framing just needs a better angle.

su-habit background: the whole-plant shot clearly shows the mounding form and lavender-blue haze but the background contains parked vehicles, a red truck/camper, and a traffic cone prominently in frame; a replacement shot in a garden or natural setting would be strongly preferable

flowers: su-flowers shows a spike with many flowers past-peak (brown spent calyces visible alongside open florets) and appears to be hand-held for the shot; a fresh fully-open spike in clean focus — showing the violet-blue tubular florets and the silver stem clearly — would be a significant upgrade

foliage: the aromatic pinnately cut silver-gray leaves are a key xeric-appeal and identification feature; they appear only as blurry background in all three shots; a close shot of a stem with leaves would serve double duty (foliage + the silver stem winter interest)
winter: plant.json has winter=true and the blurb calls out 'silver winter structure' — no winter shot exists; a late-winter shot of the silver stems before spring cut-back would complete the story

sp-flowers: the spring flower shot shows flower racemes at medium distance, but the framing is cluttered (multiple branches, dappled light, dark background) and the characteristic long pendant racemes that distinguish chokecherry are not clearly visible.
Missing fall foliage shot: the record mentions 'gold-orange fall color' but both fall shots (fa-fruit) show green or yellowing leaves — no dedicated shot shows the autumn foliage colour change.
ADD: a whole-tree habit shot showing the multi-stem tree or large shrub form — the plant ranges 12–30 ft and its architecture is not visible in any current shot.
ADD: a close-up of a single flower raceme showing the individual flowers clearly, which is the defining spring feature.

ADD (critical): a whole-tree habit shot showing the multi-stem form and overall scale — the blurb leads with 'multi-stem riparian tree' but no shot shows the full tree; all three shots are branch/canopy details
ADD: a bark close-up showing the distinctive 'smooth grey bark' called out in the blurb — a key identification feature of this species completely unrepresented
ADD: a winter shot showing the persistent woody strobiles (mentioned in care notes as a quiet winter interest feature) — no winter panel exists

Both shots are from the same Commons source (same photographer, same location, same pale-cream cultivar) — very similar images; could benefit from a second cultivar or a pink/red variety to convey the 'many cultivars' nature of climbing roses as a group
No hip/fruit shot: seasons field mentions 'hips & bare canes in winter' and wildlife notes hips feed birds; hip is a notable feature for this genus; ADD a fall/winter shot of rose hips on the canes
No foliage detail: rose leaf (pinnate, serrated, glossy) is a useful identification feature and never shown alone; minor gap given strong flower shots
Single season only (summer); the blurb mentions repeat flushes late spring–fall; a second season would strengthen the reel

Habit shot (su-habit) is a foliage-only close-up of paired round leaves with no flowers and no trellis/support in frame — does not convey 'trained climbing vine' at all; no whole-plant or vine-on-support shot exists in the reel. Needs a wider shot of the vine covering a trellis or fence, ideally with flower clusters visible. The flower shot is strong.

The seasons field mentions fluffy silver seedheads into fall — a fall seedhead shot would complete the year-round story; foliage/leaf detail is incidental in the close-up but not showcased; these are nice-to-have additions, not critical gaps.

Both shots are visually strong and clearly represent the genus well. Note: su-flower shot is a different cultivar from su-habit (flower shot = smaller double-petal pompom bloom, habit shot = Paul's Himalayan Musk on pergola with larger bloom clusters) — this is fine for a cultivar-group entry but worth noting. No foliage or cane close-up. No winter cane/hip shot (plant.json notes 'some hips & arching canes in winter' but winter interest is not illustrated). Hips mentioned as feeding birds — a hip shot would add useful fall/winter context. Seed_pods scored 1 because hips are present in neither shot but are mentioned as notable wildlife value.

su-close is a very extreme macro of open flowers — the coral-red tubes with yellow stamens are vivid and attractive but the image is cropped so tight that flower shape/context is partially lost (some flowers cut off at edges). su-structure is a strong shot: multiple clusters of coral tubes on the vine with the distinctive perfoliate leaves visible — good bloom-in-context coverage. Gaps: (1) No whole-vine/whole-plant shot showing how the twining vine covers a trellis or fence — essential for a vine entry; scored 0 for whole_plant. (2) The perfoliate (clasping) leaves are a key ID feature for this species; they appear in su-structure but are not featured clearly. (3) No fall/berry shot (red berries noted in blurb as a wildlife feature; winter: true). (4) su-close framing is very tight and could benefit from slightly pulling back to show the full nodding cluster. Adding a wide vine-on-support shot and a berry/fall shot would significantly strengthen this entry.
